Description: Tendency to search for, interpret, and remember information that confirms pre-existing beliefs while ignoring contradictory evidence.
Impact on Problem Solving: Leads to incomplete analysis; Kills hypothesis testing; Results in overconfident wrong answers; Prevents learning from data
How to Detect: You only look for supporting evidence; You dismiss data that contradicts your view; You feel very certain despite mixed evidence; Your analysis confirms your initial guess
Debiasing Strategy: Assign a red team to argue the opposite case; Pre-commit to what evidence would change your mind; Actively seek disconfirming data first; Use structured analysis that forces consideration of alternatives
Example: A team convinced their product is best ignores customer complaints and competitor reviews, focusing only on positive testimonials

Keywords: anchor, first-impression, starting-point, adjustment, initial-estimate, reference-point
Description: Over-relying on the first piece of information encountered (the anchor) when making judgments, with insufficient adjustment from that anchor.
Impact on Problem Solving: Initial framing dominates final answer; Market sizing anchored on first number heard; Negotiations anchored by first offer; Estimates cluster around arbitrary starting points
How to Detect: Your estimate is suspiciously close to the first number you saw; You struggle to move far from an initial reference; Different starting points lead to different answers for the same question
Debiasing Strategy: Generate your own estimate before seeing others; Use multiple independent starting points; Consider the problem from scratch; Explicitly test extreme values; Use base rates as anchor instead of anecdotes
Example: When asked if a project will cost more or less than $10M, teams tend to estimate near $10M regardless of actual scope. Starting with $1M or $100M would yield very different answers

Keywords: availability, recent, vivid, memorable, frequency-estimation, salience
Description: Judging the likelihood of events based on how easily examples come to mind. Recent, vivid, or emotionally charged events seem more probable.
Impact on Problem Solving: Overweighting recent events in analysis; Misjudging risk (terrorism vs car accidents); Overreacting to vivid failures; Ignoring base rates; Recency bias in trend analysis
How to Detect: Your risk assessment mirrors recent news; You weight vivid anecdotes over statistics; You think something is common because you can easily recall examples
Debiasing Strategy: Use base rate statistics rather than examples; Systematically collect data rather than relying on memory; Ask 'what does the data actually show?' not 'what examples can I think of?'; Consult actuarial tables for risk
Example: After a high-profile security breach, company vastly overinvests in security while ignoring the statistically much larger risk of employee turnover

Keywords: sunk-cost, escalation, commitment, invested, cant-stop-now, doubling-down
Description: Continuing a course of action because of previously invested resources (time, money, effort) rather than future value. 'We have come too far to stop now.'
Impact on Problem Solving: Prevents killing bad projects; Throws good money after bad; Delays pivoting; Makes exit decisions emotional rather than analytical; Escalation of commitment
How to Detect: You justify continuing because of past investment; The phrase 'we have already spent X' comes up; Switching costs are framed as losses not investments; The project wouldnt be approved if starting fresh today
Debiasing Strategy: Ask: 'If we were starting from scratch today, would we choose this path?'; Evaluate based only on future costs and benefits; Set pre-commitment kill criteria; Get outside perspective; Frame as new investment decision
Example: A company continues developing a product for 2 more years because theyve already spent $5M, even though market research shows low demand. The $5M is gone regardless.

Keywords: groupthink, conformity, consensus, dissent, cohesion, pressure, agreement
Description: Desire for group harmony leads to irrational or dysfunctional decision-making. Dissent is suppressed. The group converges on a comfortable answer.
Impact on Problem Solving: Kills diverse thinking; Prevents challenge of bad ideas; Creates false confidence; Missed risks; Premature consensus without thorough analysis
How to Detect: Everyone agrees too quickly; No one plays devils advocate; Dissenting voices go quiet; The group pressures outliers; Decision feels unanimous without real debate
Debiasing Strategy: Assign a devils advocate role (rotating); Use anonymous input before discussion; Bring in external perspectives; Leader speaks last; Encourage and reward constructive dissent; Pre-mortem exercise
Example: A board unanimously approves an acquisition without anyone raising concerns about integration risks, because the CEO is visibly enthusiastic and no one wants to appear unsupportive

Keywords: overconfidence, certainty, calibration, range, prediction, hubris, precision
Description: Systematic overestimation of the accuracy of ones own judgments and predictions. Confidence intervals are too narrow. Experts are often the most overconfident.
Impact on Problem Solving: Underestimates uncertainty; Narrow confidence intervals; Overcommits to single forecast; Ignores alternative outcomes; Takes excessive risk; Kills contingency planning
How to Detect: Your predictions are precise but often wrong; Your confidence intervals are too narrow; You dismiss low-probability events; You dont update beliefs when evidence contradicts
Debiasing Strategy: Use explicit confidence intervals; Track prediction accuracy over time; Widen ranges by default; Use reference class forecasting; Pre-mortem analysis; Assign probabilities and calibrate
Example: An executive predicts revenue of exactly $50M next year with 95% confidence. Historically, their predictions are off by 30%+ half the time. Calibrated range would be $35-65M

Keywords: status-quo, inertia, default, change-aversion, do-nothing, resistance
Description: Preference for the current state of affairs. Change is treated as risky while doing nothing is treated as safe, even when doing nothing carries risk.
Impact on Problem Solving: Blocks innovation; Prevents necessary changes; Overvalues current approach; Fails to consider opportunity cost of inaction; Default option gets unfair advantage
How to Detect: Doing nothing is framed as safe; Change is scrutinized more than continuation; You cant articulate why the current approach is best; The cost of switching is emphasized but the cost of staying is not
Debiasing Strategy: Frame the decision as a new choice (not change vs stay); Quantify the cost of inaction; Ask 'if we werent already doing this, would we start?'; Evaluate all options on equal footing including status quo
Example: A company continues using legacy software because migrating seems risky, while the cost of maintaining the old system exceeds migration costs over 3 years

Keywords: framing, presentation, context, wording, perspective, loss-aversion, reference
Description: Decisions are influenced by how information is presented rather than its objective content. Same data framed as gain vs loss produces different decisions.
Impact on Problem Solving: How the problem is framed determines which solutions are considered; Loss framing causes risk aversion; Gain framing causes risk seeking; Narrow framing misses options
How to Detect: The same data leads to different conclusions depending on presentation; Stakeholders react differently to identical information framed differently; You havent tried reframing the problem
Debiasing Strategy: Deliberately reframe the problem from multiple perspectives; Present data as both gain and loss; Challenge the initial frame; Use absolute numbers alongside percentages; Test multiple framings with different audiences
Example: A product with 95% satisfaction sounds great. The same product with 1 in 20 customers unhappy sounds concerning. Same data, different frame, different reaction

Keywords: survivorship, selection, hidden-data, missing-data, successful-only, dropout
Description: Drawing conclusions only from visible successes while ignoring the invisible failures. Like studying only successful companies to find success factors.
Impact on Problem Solving: Overly optimistic conclusions; Missing critical failure factors; Bad strategy from biased sample; Underestimating risk; 'If they can do it so can we' fallacy
How to Detect: Your data only includes survivors/successes; You havent looked at failures; Your conclusion is 'do what successful X did'; You cant explain why some who did the same thing failed
Debiasing Strategy: Actively seek data on failures; Ask 'how many tried this and failed?'; Include dropouts/failures in analysis; Use proper control groups; Compare successful and unsuccessful cases side by side
Example: Studying 50 successful startups to find common traits (vision, persistence, risk-taking) without studying the 500 failed startups that had the same traits

Keywords: planning, optimism, timeline, budget, underestimate, over-promise, schedule
Description: Systematic tendency to underestimate time, costs, and risks while overestimating benefits. Projects consistently exceed budget and timeline.
Impact on Problem Solving: Over-promising and under-delivering; Budget overruns; Timeline slippage; Inadequate resource allocation; Unrealistic commitments
How to Detect: Your estimates are consistently too optimistic; You focus on the plan rather than reference class of similar past projects; Best case scenario is treated as base case
Debiasing Strategy: Use reference class forecasting (how long did similar projects actually take?); Add systematic buffer (multiply by 1.5-3x); Track actual vs estimated over time; Break into smaller pieces and estimate each; Use pre-mortem to identify what could go wrong
Example: A software project estimated at 6 months and $500K is completed in 14 months at $1.2M. The team cited unique challenges, but this pattern repeats on every project

Keywords: dunning-kruger, competence, metacognition, expertise, novice, confidence-gap
Description: People with limited knowledge overestimate their competence, while experts often underestimate theirs. The less you know, the less you know what you dont know.
Impact on Problem Solving: Novices propose overly simple solutions; Expert caution is dismissed; Team composition misjudges capabilities; Overconfident scoping of unfamiliar work
How to Detect: Juniors are very confident about complex topics; Experts express nuanced uncertainty; People dismiss complexity in domains they havent studied deeply
Debiasing Strategy: Seek feedback from genuine experts; Recognize that feeling confident doesnt mean being competent; Value epistemic humility; Test competence with specific non-trivial questions; Red team proposals
Example: A team with no ML experience estimates building a recommendation engine will take 2 weeks. The ML expert estimates 3 months. Leadership trusts the confident estimate

Keywords: narrative, story, pattern, causation, hindsight, explanation, post-hoc
Description: Tendency to create coherent narratives from random events. We see patterns where none exist and create causal stories for coincidences.
Impact on Problem Solving: Over-interpreting data; Seeing false patterns; Creating causal stories from correlations; Hindsight bias ('it was obvious'); Oversimplifying complex situations
How to Detect: The explanation sounds like a neat story; The narrative ignores contradictory evidence; The causal chain seems inevitable in hindsight; You found one pattern and stopped looking for alternatives
Debiasing Strategy: Test causal claims with data; Look for alternative explanations; Be suspicious of neat narratives; Distinguish correlation from causation; Use statistical tests not just stories; Consider base rates
Example: The story of a companys success focuses on the founders vision and pivots, ignoring the role of timing, luck, and market conditions. The same narrative could describe 100 failed companies
